## **The Future of Supply Chain: Emerging Technologies You Need to Know**

# Emerging technologies like AI, IoT, blockchain, etc., are revolutionizing supply chain visibility, efficiency, and resilience. Here’s what’s coming next.

### **In Brief**

The future of supply chain management looks vastly different than the past. Emerging technologies are dramatically transforming processes and operations across procurement, production, and distribution. 

Strategic leaders must understand how artificial intelligence, the internet of things, blockchain, and machine learning specifically improve agility, visibility, and efficiencies. Additionally, embracing digital transformation requires new collaborative mindsets and adapting workforce talents. 

Staying ahead involves actively scanning the horizon for disruptive innovations such as digital twins and autonomous drones. Today’s article explores the key technical advances revolutionizing supply chains and what steps organizations should take to ensure readiness for industry evolution.

---

### **Key Technologies Revolutionizing the Supply Chain**

* [Artificial Intelligence (AI)](#1)
* [Internet of Things (IoT)](#2)
* [Blockchain](#3)
* [Machine Learning](#4)

Let’s explore each in detail. 

---

### Table of contents:

* [Implementing Digital Transformation in Supply Chains](#5)
* [Additional Emerging Technologies](#6)

#### **The key technologies and their valuable aspects**

| **Technology** | **Applications** | **Valuable Aspects** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Artificial Intelligence (AI) | Demand forecasting, workflow automation, anomaly detection | Enhances efficiency, reduces errors, optimizes logistics |
| Internet of Things (IoT) | Real-time tracking, automated warehouse management, predictive maintenance | Improves visibility, reduces downtime, enhances productivity |
| Blockchain | Smart contracts, transparent record-keeping, fraud prevention | Increases transparency, ensures data security, reduces costs |
| Machine Learning (ML) | Predictive analytics, demand planning, risk assessment | Enables data-driven decisions, improves forecasting accuracy |

### **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**

Artificial intelligence assists businesses in [tracking inventory levels](https://binmile.com/blog/ai-inventory-management/) and demand patterns. AI systems analyze past sales data to forecast future requirements accurately. Predictive analytics help companies order their products in suitable quantities from manufacturers. It prevents stock-outs and reduces storage of excess stock.

[AI automates routine supply chain](https://business.linkedin.com/content/dam/me/business/en-us/marketing-solutions/case-studies/pdfs/AI-in-B2B.pdf) activities. Robots and smart software perform repetitive tasks like picking, packing, shipping, and transportation. AI finds efficiencies by optimizing workflows. It schedules production and routes shipments utilizing real-time traffic and weather data. 

AI-powered chatbots and virtual agents answer vendor queries fast. They provide round-the-clock support for procurement professionals and suppliers on pricing, order status or shipment delays. 

AI detects anomalies in orders, invoices, or deliveries early on. It flags deviations from standard procedures for human review. This helps managers troubleshoot issues and find solutions rapidly. Overall, [AI brings consistency, speed and intelligently](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/ai-azure-ai-services-blog/supply-chain-anomaly-detection-and-root-cause-analysis-with/ba-p/2871920) handles high volumes across vast supplier networks for businesses.

AI benefits B2B companies by streamlining supply chain processes end-to-end. It frees up resources from repetitive work. Importantly, AI helps businesses, and their vendors make well-informed decisions to operate more productively with fewer errors or delays.

---

### **Internet of Things (IoT)**

IoT connects physical objects like sensors to the internet, enabling them to collect and exchange data. This allows AI to analyze real-time information from supply chain operations.

#### ***Product and Asset Tracking***

IoT sensors [track inventory, equipment](https://www.digiteum.com/iot-supply-chain/), and shipments continuously. [RFID/NFC tags](https://www.hologram.io/blog/10-best-iot-asset-tracking-systems/) attached to items share identifiers, location, and status wirelessly. AI spot anomalies to optimize workflows and flag issues proactively. This visibility enhances resource allocation and maintenance planning.

#### ***Automated Warehouse Management***

 Drones and AI-assisted robots perform picking, packing and palletizing operations in touchless warehouses. Computer vision guides vehicles to retrieve stock accurately as per orders. AI directs the fleet optimally to improve productivity and reduce costs.

#### ***Predictive Maintenance***

Sensors in machines and vehicles feed operating parameters to [AI models](https://a-team.global/blog/iot-predictive-maintenance/). These analyze usage patterns and predict breakdowns before occurrences. Scheduled servicing based on predictive alerts raises equipment uptime significantly.

#### ***Digital Twins and Simulation***

Real-time IoT data generates digital replicas of physical operations. AI simulates ‘what-if’ scenarios on these virtual twins to test changes, minimize risks and costs before implementations on actual systems.

---

### **Blockchain**

Blockchain revolutionizes how companies manage supply chains. Using blockchain, [suppliers and buyers directly interact through smart contracts](https://www.ibm.com/blockchain-supply-chain). This removes middlemen and unnecessary costs while improving transparency.

When a supplier ships an order, the details automatically enter an immutable record visible to all participating stakeholders. Payments are released promptly after verification of delivery. Disputes are rare, because there are no intermediaries to blur the lines of responsibility.

According to [HBR](https://hbr.org/2020/05/building-a-transparent-supply-chain), traceability maximizes throughout the supply chain. Partners track exact locations and conditions of goods in real-time. This ensures quality and prevents theft or spoilage. Customers appreciate full visibility into origins and handling of what they purchase.

To adopt blockchain, companies work with specialized technology providers. Suppliers and buyers install compatible software letting them issue shipments, payments, and inspections on the shared platform. New members easily onboard after validating identities once.

Network effects grow as more participate. Individual businesses reduce risks and expenses coordinating independently. Confidence strengthens among all counterparts accustomed to dealing straightforwardly, efficiently, and openly on blockchain. The innovative system establishes reliable, straightforward processes future-proofing supply chain management for all involved.

---

### **Machine Learning**

Machine learning impacts supply chain management in important ways. It allows supply chain managers to make better predictions. [ML models analyze massive amounts of data](https://hbr.org/2024/03/how-machine-learning-will-transform-supply-chain-management) from past supply chain operations. They find hidden patterns human minds cannot see.

The models learn from this data. They help forecast demand and plan productions schedules more accurately. Managers gain insights into which products sell best and when. ML also optimizes supply chain networks. 

It recommends ideal inventory levels, transport routes, and facility locations to lower costs. According to [Hindawi](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/mpe/2021/1476043/), Supply chain managers can implement ML models in their own operations. 

First, they have to collect and organize historic data on sales, inventory, shipping etc. This ‘trains’ the ML model to understand patterns.Next, managers input new data and the model predicts outcomes. Its results help managers make informed logistics decisions for placing orders, allocating resources, and managing unforeseen disruptions.

Regularly feeding updated data maintains the model’s accuracy over time. Managers leverage ML models’ abilities but still oversee crucial duties. By intelligently applying these automated learning systems, businesses future-proof supply chain operations for consistent efficiency and customer satisfaction through tomorrow’s unpredictable changes.

---

## **Implementing Digital Transformation in Supply Chains**

Implementing successful digital transformation in supply chain operations requires strategic planning, enhancing agility through collaboration, investing in human talent, and staying ahead of technological and methodological trends. Supply chain leaders that take a structured approach to leveraging advanced capabilities can gain tremendous competitive advantages.

### **Strategic Planning**

The first critical step is developing a comprehensive strategic plan for digitally upgrading end-to-end supply chain processes. It entails clearly defining current shortcomings and future objectives, auditing existing procedures and assets to identity digitization opportunities, researching relevant technologies and solutions, building executable roadmaps aligning to overarching corporate goals, and establishing success metrics. Additional vital components include:

* Performing cost-benefit analyses to concentrate resources on transformations projected to drive maximum financial returns or other defined strategic value;
* Assigning specialized task forces with technical and business expertise to thoroughly evaluate use cases;
* Modeling multiple implementation scenarios through proof-of-concept pilot testing;
* Cultivating buy-in and budget commitment from leadership stakeholders.

Meticulous planning reduces deployment risks and ensures that technology integrations contribute to clearly defined supply chain objectives in terms of efficiency, visibility, accountability, etc.

### **Agility and Collaboration**

Digital levers should focus on dramatically amplifying organizational agility. Supply chains face innate challenges from siloed functions, complex partner networks and reliance on legacy procedures. 

Modern solutions centered on data integrations, cloud-based control towers and decision-making algorithms cultivate previously impossible flexibility, responsiveness, and resilience. You can use the following to achieve more efficiency:

* Tightening connections with external suppliers, OEMs, logistics providers and retailers using API data exchanges to enable real-time transparency, risk detection and collaborative resolutions;
* Investing in modular architecture emphasizing compartmentalization and loose couplings to isolate downstream disruptions;
* Deploying interactive Gantt chart tools for dynamic adjustments of interdependent distribution timelines in fluid situations;
* Modeling a breadth of disruption scenarios to create contingency protocols engaging both internal groups and external partners;

### **Investing in Talent**

Transforming supply chains without transforming employee skill sets invites failure. Organizations must become talent incubators through extensive reskilling/upskilling initiatives if they hope to extract full value from bleeding-edge technologies. Several impactful approaches include:

* Automating repetitive entry-level jobs to redirect personnel toward value-added analytics and innovation;
* Incentivizing interests in high-priority competency domains through tuition reimbursement programs;
* Curating video tutorials, online courses and microlearning content to foster self-education;
* Subsidizing nanodegrees, workshops, and professional certifications;
* Building cross-department rotations to spur understanding of integrated systems vs linear sequences;

Nurturing purposeful internal capabilities to harness and enhance technological functionalities breeds true differentiation. No quantity of technology outweighs human creativity.

### **Staying Ahead of Trends**

Change remains the supply chain’s sole constant. Complacency kills. Best-in-class players perpetually scan horizons for emerging innovations in logistics hardware, protocols and methodologies – then run realistic trial implementations to gauge strategic importance.

Farsighted organizations actively experiment with bleeding-edge robotics, autonomous freight vehicles, automated picking tools and augmented reality teleassistance to evaluate operational impacts.

You can closely track blockchain evolutions across financial settlement, chain of custody documentation, sensor-driven monitoring and more. Make sure you continuously engage analytics solution providers to rapidly pilot cutting-edge forecasting algorithms as they emerge. Consider gradual transitions toward circular economy “reuse and recycle” models and assess necessary operational alterations.

Keeping technological functionality flexible allows smoothly folding high-potential advances into existing environments before rivals. Supply chain transformation is an endless pursuit – not a one-time initiative.

---

## **Additional Emerging Technologies**

### **Digital Twins**

Digital twins create virtual simulated environments mirroring real-world physical assets and processes. Sophisticated physics engines update digital clone behaviors dynamically based on live data streams from corresponding machinery. Supply chain leaders leverage these cyber representations to safely optimize workings through low-risk virtual testing before costly physical implementations.

### **Drones**

Unmanned autonomous drones promise to revolutionize inventory handling and last-mile transportation. Aerial vehicles access hard-to-reach warehouse locations speedily to lift heavy pallets or deliver small parcels. 

Onboard cameras confirm picking accuracy as robot arms gently secure items. Drones will prove integral for rapid delivery in congested urban zones or following natural disasters when roads become unpassable.

### **Self-Driving Vehicles**

Self-driving trucks traveling continuously without human operators will dominate long-haul logistics. Savings from reduced labor costs and increased trailer utilization rates via faster rides are substantial. Automakers already testing prototypes still need to demonstrate required safety. But once refined, autonomous big rigs will transform hub-to-hub distribution economics.

### **Exoskeletons**

Powered ergonomic exoskeletons worn by warehouse workers enhance strength, speed and stamina when lifting, pulling, and manipulating items. These bionic suits prevent repetitive strain injuries from frequent materials handling motions. Businesses trialing prototypes report exceptional productivity gains plus overwhelmingly positive feedback regarding comfort.

### **Augmented Reality**

Future workers wearing augmented reality glasses receive real-time guidance on optimally fulfilling duties. For example, computer vision overlays highlight proper selection points or project holographic models demonstrating ideal packing arrangements. Hands-free audio instructions also Provide step-by-step directionality. This technology promises to improve training efficiency and decision support.

### **Additive Manufacturing**

3D printing revolutionizes replacement parts logistics through on-demand fabrication. Rather than maintaining slow-moving component inventory, businesses simply store digital blueprints then print items as needed on-site via extrusion. Although initially supplementing existing scale production, this technology may eventually enable highly distributed hyper-local manufacturing.

---

## **Final Words**

The pace of innovation reshaping supply chain operations generates tremendous opportunities alongside daunting complexity for leaders aiming to futureproof competitiveness. 

While emerging capabilities like additive manufacturing, exoskeletons and autonomous drones showcase tantalizing potentials, companies must avoid overextending resources chasing too many disparate pilot projects simultaneously. 

Strategic focus matters because digital transformation requires carefully prioritizing high-impact opportunities delivering compelling value in areas like visibility, accountability, flexibility, and sustainability. But only a sharp emphasis on cultivating complementary talent and an experimental learning culture will unlock lasting wins.

FAQs

### **How can API-based integrations streamline our workflows with suppliers and partners?**

Modern integration platforms leverage APIs to seamlessly link ordering systems, logistics trackers, inventory databases and more between companies. This real-time data synchronization saves manual efforts re-entering information and eliminates delays waiting for batch exports.

### **Why is blockchain valuable for bringing transparency across our complex supplier network?**

Blockchain’s distributed encrypted ledger establishes a single shared record of transactions, shipments, payments etc. directly between businesses. All actions become verifiable and traceable back to their origins without obfuscation from intermediaries.

### **How can predictive analytics tools improve the accuracy of our B2B demand forecasts?**

By analyzing large volumes of past sales data, AI-based statistical and machine learning models detect subtle demand patterns difficult for humans to manually identify. These insights enhance supply/production planning and inventory management.

### **What benefits can IoT-enabled asset tracking offer our business partners?**

Connected IoT sensors provide 24/7 equipment statuses and movements to partners in real-time. This unprecedented visibility facilitates smarter utilization, security, and predictive maintenance across distributed corporate assets.

### **How can big data analytics optimize production volumes with uneven B2B purchasing cycles?**

Sophisticated analytics translate immense information on segmented buyer behaviors into granular insights on fluctuating purchase frequencies, magnitudes, and seasonal impacts. These support highly tailored forecasting by product lines.

### **Why migrate our supply chain software solutions to the cloud versus continuing on-premises models?**

Cloud-native solutions centralize data across the supply chain for improved analytics while enabling access from anywhere. Cloud platforms lower IT costs by automating maintenance and scaling elastically to usage spikes.

### **How can machine learning enhance inventory planning in complex B2B supply chain environments?**

By processing volumes of historical data, ML models detect subtle demand patterns. As new sales data enters regularly, algorithms self-adjust projections to minimize inventory misalignments like stock-outs or excesses.

# Best supply chain technologies

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='266'%20width='497'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)
![Author: George Maksimenko]()
![Author: George Maksimenko](https://images.ctfassets.net/lyp5g4h7vq5n/3s5cVeMjSGJaiC9LBflFva/15991635f78f108ebf465d07857b8adb/IMG_1975_004.jpg?w=497&h=266&fl=progressive&q=100&fm=jpg)

The supply chain tech market has been evolving rapidly in recent years. Numerous software and hardware companies are entering the market with supply chain technology, which is bringing about a major shift in business operations.

Real entrepreneurship is driving supply chain technology trends, but it's still questionable whether new technologies can truly support your business. For experts in logistics and the supply chain market, innovations always bring uncertainty. This is the reason why many future technologies still require years before they will be fully adaptable in the supply chain.

We will explain the tech supply chain and how new supply chain technology can bring positive change to your business without disrupting your current operations. Frankly speaking, what deserves attention is how companies should provide solutions without interrupting ongoing operations. Let's examine technologies in supply chain management closely and investigate where actual efficiency can be found.

## **Getting on the journey with supply chain technology**

While the topic of this article promises to deliver more about emerging technologies, it's important to note that changes in the supply chain don't happen overnight. Companies with well-established business processes will use extensive procurement to adapt to [supply chain digital transformation](/blog/supply-chain-digital-transformation/) technologies. Sometimes, it can take a few years before one goes live.

On the other hand, small and medium-sized businesses will seek cost-efficient and faster solutions to comply with these technologies, especially if their procurement processes aren't entirely in place. They may require assistance in choosing the right partner. The deployment process for supply chain technologies remains much shorter.

In every scenario, **companies will question which supply chain management technology is most suitable for them**, especially with numerous emerging options. Companies will review long- and short-term advantages and decide on their business goals, financial ability, integrations, ease of use, and how those technologies will affect their sustainable supply chains.

## **Top 5 technologies in supply chain management**

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='718'%20width='1920'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Technologies used in supply chain management]()
![Technologies used in supply chain management](https://images.ctfassets.net/lyp5g4h7vq5n/5JvM0Dd2mwbCWhPkMi2qhw/888ea3efcc5b178dbd0330bd5915ad95/Top_5_technologies_in_supply_chain_management.png?w=1920&h=718&q=50&fm=png)

### **1. Internet of things**

For many small and medium-sized companies, IoT remains a distant vision in business evolution. Companies already existing on the market and remaining SMBs are struggling with many issues IoT can solve in the transportation, supply chain, and logistics industries. That matter of fact is to find a place for the successful application of IoT cost-effectively. Companies such as Volvo, the car producer belong to leading companies in this area, and have already implemented IoT solutions in their supply chains, using these technologies to streamline operations. They have actually used IoT to increase the traceability of spare parts.

[**McKinsey research**](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/iot-value-set-to-accelerate-through-2030-where-and-how-to-capture-it) **predicted that by 2030, the application of IoT in supply chain management (SCM) could enable $5.5 trillion to $12.6 trillion in value globally.** This includes the value captured by consumers and customers of IoT products and services.

The numbers are pumped up artificially, but we can be sure that there are low-hanging fruits for supply chains that want to gain a competitive advantage with low investment. Why low investment? It's because, despite many companies still recognizing technology adaptation as an expensive investment, it can be easier with custom software development.

Custom software development solutions and investment in IoT don't have to consume many resources. Especially when it comes to implementation based on MVP. We've learned from our experience that it's the most suitable way of adapting new technologies, such as IoT, for small and medium-sized businesses.

You can, for example, start small to see the pain points of the entire project. You can decide if the solution is suitable for now or just leave it in the experimental phase to keep a competitive advantage in your offering for the future.

That's right. Being an early adopter of new technologies allows you to use it as a competitive advantage in your offering when it comes to contracting new customers. Simply put, companies just are more eager to work with innovative businesses.

**Here is an example of use cases of IoT in the food supply chain:**

Significant advances in food monitoring technologies in supply chains have been made in recent years. The evolution has been important, from basic labeling systems such as barcodes to advanced Industry 4.0 solutions integrating AI, IoT, big data, sensors, and blockchain.

For example, sensors can now monitor grain stored in silos. In real-time, they track vital factors such as temperature, moisture, and air quality. It ensures optimal conditions to prevent mold, bacteria, and insect damage. IoT systems enable remote monitoring and automated alerts based on pre-set thresholds.

### **2. Artificial intelligence**

We must stick to AI in our Top 5 tech in SCM as it is rapidly evolving, particularly considering that a lot of manual work in the Supply Chain will likely be removed within a decade. An easy way of adapting **AI has been to point out that its usage has increased by 270% within the last three years!**

Speaking about [AI agents](/ai-agent-development-services/) and apps, we need to mention the benefits of early adopters again. [According to McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/metals-and-mining/our-insights/succeeding-in-the-ai-supply-chain-revolution), successfully implementing **AI-enabled supply-chain management has enabled early adopters to improve logistics costs** by 15 percent, inventory levels by 35 percent, and service levels by 65 percent compared to market competitors.

Need help with artificial intelligence software development?

Learn how we can boost your logistics processes

Another useful hint is that the global market for AI in the supply chain industry is expected to be over [$20 billion by 2028](https://www.businessdit.com/ai-in-supply-chain/), with a Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of 20.5%. Well, it's hard to neglect such a factor.

We have steadily observed that companies are increasingly willing to integrate artificial intelligence into their supply chains. However, they remain very cautious regarding AI as something that can fully replace their traditional business approaches.

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='665'%20width='1920'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Potential AI challenges in the supply chain]()
![Potential AI challenges in the supply chain](https://images.ctfassets.net/lyp5g4h7vq5n/3jPQe3UbL9l6ZRuhb22mbE/0576fb5ccbc51105dd4ab0458eafb875/A.I._in_the_supply_chain.png?w=1920&h=665&q=50&fm=png)

**We observe companies are cautious about AI in the supply chain for three main reasons:**

**Complexity.**  A.I. systems can introduce complexity into supply chain processes, making them harder to understand and manage.

**Dependency risk.** Excessive reliance on AI without adequate fallback mechanisms can lead to vulnerabilities. If AI systems fail or encounter errors, they can disrupt supply chain operations.

**Data security concerns.** Artificial intelligence heavily relies on data. Therefore, it's clear that any breaches or compromises in data security could expose sensitive supply chain information.

**Two examples of artificial intelligence in the supply chain:**

**Demand forecasting and planning.** Artificial intelligence is powering forecasting engines, enabling interactive analysis of shipping data. U.S. industrial goods distributors use A.I. solutions to move toward dynamic planning and improve collaboration with suppliers and retailers.

**Inventory management.** Artificial intelligence improves logistics management optimizes inventory turnover and reduces inventory shortages. With an A.I. solution, supply chain logistics companies can reduce inventory by 43%. Artificial intelligence often employs more advanced technologies, such as drones, to photograph inventory in warehouses and obtain a clear view of real inventory.

### **3. Augmented data Intelligence and data analytics**

The phrase sounds awkward, but it has become a significant trend in supply chain technologies because an augmented approach is needed to get the most out of the market. You cannot rely on partial data; you can employ some statistics to gain a clear and holistic perspective on forecasting demand. So, Data Intelligence has now evolved into Augmented Data Intelligence.

What does this mean? Frankly speaking, it's about improving continuous decision-making in supply chains. Companies are integrating multiple technologies to enhance data intelligence. Together, they speed up sophisticated data processing and provide valuable insights, forecasts, and recommendations.

Augmented Data Intelligence is about powering up data and analytics technology, which is based on platforms these days. [**According to data from Gartner**](https://www.gartner.com/en/topics/data-and-analytics)**, they expect that by 2025, 70% of organizations will be compelled to shift their focus** from big to small and comprehensive data to leverage available data more effectively. It's all about reducing the required volume or extracting more value from unstructured, diverse data sources. So, again, a good data source is about data source integration.

From firsthand experience, we know that our customers in the supply chain heavily depend on quality data. We always provide this assurance to our customers by focusing on developing exact solutions based on quality written code. It is crucial when developing customized [software solutions for supply chain](/supply-chain-software-development/) companies that depend on data.

**A good example of our involvement in such a project is our participation in a** [**shipping document management platform**](/case-studies/blockchain-backed-shipping-documentation-platform/) **project**, where data management is based on workflows that rely on slick code. By using common sense when participating in any project, we know that this is a way to minimize the risk of unforeseen events and problems with the continued performance of the platform.

**Examples of augmented data Intelligence and data analytics in the supply chain:**

In supply chain management (SCM), analytics provides valuable information that enables organizations to make strategic decisions. You can optimize processes, reduce costs, improve visibility, and create a more efficient and responsive supply chain.

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='718'%20width='1920'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Traditional augmented data intelligence analytics types ]()
![Traditional augmented data intelligence analytics types ](https://images.ctfassets.net/lyp5g4h7vq5n/77TxHnEZSfNc33kR8BTrEj/feea436580c7efd2e7d03f0b10cc5858/Data_Intelligence_analytics.png?w=1920&h=718&q=50&fm=png)

**Traditional augmented data Intelligence analytics, performed by analytical software platforms, can be broadly categorized into four types:**

**Descriptive analytics.** Describes past events and summarizes historical data to provide insights and trends. Examples can be found in the supply chain's sales and financial reports.

**Diagnostic analytics.** Its purpose is to examine why something happened in the past. This type of analysis attempts to identify the causes of certain events or patterns. Examples include root cause analysis and performance analysis.

**Predictive analytics.** Its goal is to predict future performance based on historical data using statistical algorithms and machine learning models. Examples include sales forecasting and demand prediction.

**Prescriptive analytics.** Recommends actions to optimize results by combining historical data, predictive models, and decision analysis. This provides decision support systems, logistics technology, and optimization models.

### **4. Warehouse automation**

Moving around the U.S. or E.U. market, we encounter many companies already embracing automation in warehouses and making it a reality. It's just one of the many trends already in implementation. However, it never stands alone; IoT, analytics, and artificial intelligence often complement it.

According to [McKinsey data](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/operations/our-insights), the overall automation market is growing rapidly. Some expert sources predict that robot shipments will increase by up to 50 percent each year through 2030. It translates to warehouse automation growing by more than 10 percent annually, which is remarkable when considering the global scale.

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='534'%20width='1000'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Digital transformations in warehousing and supply chain]()
![Digital transformations in warehousing and supply chain](https://images.ctfassets.net/lyp5g4h7vq5n/1NmxWmZVnZNwaBpLXqvJI7/4b1a3aa1e2d86e2f4bbdf2027c1768cb/Digital_transformations_in_warehousing_and_supply_chain.png?w=1000&h=534&q=50&fm=png)

Digital transformations in warehousing and supply chain

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='500'%20width='500'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Digital transformations in warehousing and supply chain]()
![Digital transformations in warehousing and supply chain](https://images.ctfassets.net/lyp5g4h7vq5n/2ui0NtphESp4DTwrqJTVu2/3f2aeb8b17112cf84d8c9433355f8a2a/pawel-bes-min.jpg?w=500&h=500&fl=progressive&q=50&fm=jpg)

Custom software development is crucial in this matter. We have seen this through years of cooperation with supply chain companies that directly integrate their warehouses with supply chain operations. We observe this widely among companies that are implementing [warehouse management systems](/warehouse-management-system-development-services/) and [fleet management systems](/fleet-management-systems-development-services/) together.

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='860.9999999999999'%20width='1980'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Warehouse automation in the supply chain]()
![Warehouse automation in the supply chain](https://images.ctfassets.net/lyp5g4h7vq5n/33U86mDUvEVmCgPQDR1e5x/5c3bdf25e85ca07def276d1ffd7cedc8/warehouse_automation.png?w=1980&h=861&q=50&fm=png)

**Example of warehouse automation in the supply chain:**

**WMS automation.** These automations mainly occur in Warehouse Management System (WMS) software. It covers tasks such as automatic inventory tracking and shipping rates.

**Machine learning.** Using artificial intelligence (AI) programs and algorithms, machine learning improves specific warehouse management functions.

**Collaborative robots.** These robots, also known as co-bots, are designed to collaborate with workers. They are responsible for increasing efficiency and productivity while minimizing errors.

**Storage and retrieval systems.** Using machines such as conveyor belts and product lifters, companies can store and retrieve products from inventory.

**Wearable technology.** Smart glasses and similar products integrate augmented reality to help maintenance teams and inventory workers navigate warehouses more efficiently.

**Predictive maintenance automation.** Monitoring internal and external systems constantly. These automation detect potential problems and make quick repairs to prevent costly downtime.

### **5. Robotics in logistics technology**

Integrating robots into logistics enhances the speed and accuracy of supply chain operations while minimizing human error. Robots outperform workers in terms of work time and productivity.

A significant advantage for companies is replacing human manual work, thereby increasing overall productivity with data analytics-based accurate measurements. Automating robotic processes enables the supply chain to decrease human involvement and identify and eliminate inefficiencies throughout the network.

Similar to warehouse automation, **Robotic process automation (RPA) facilitates smooth operations around the clock**, enabling managers to continuously optimize efficiency. With the help of artificial intelligence, RPA is similar to warehouse automation.

According to a McKinsey survey, 23% of supply chain respondents plan to invest more than $500 million in automation and robotics. [The 2022 McKinsey Global Industrial Robotics Survey](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/industrials-and-electronics/our-insights/unlocking-the-industrial-potential-of-robotics-and-automation) indicates that among the industrial sectors surveyed, retail and consumer goods are expected to be the largest automation spenders over the next five years, with the automotive industry accounting for 8% of the total.

Automation and robotization strictly depend on custom software. We know what kind of investment is required. **Here are factors that improve robots with custom software based on our internal experience:**

**Examples of robot applications in the supply chain:**

One of the most hazardous tasks in distribution centers involves racking, staging, and placing pallets on racks, posing significant risks to employees. To mitigate these dangers, robots have been introduced to take over these roles.

A perfect example is an intelligent robot with vision, machine learning, artificial intelligence, and grasping technology. This robot can automatically load and unload pallets without the need for pre-programming. It can also reduce the need for workers to engage in hazardous tasks such as stacking pallets in racks or stacking and placing pallets on racks.

Automated storage and retrieval systems that use artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning algorithms can accurately process orders without human intervention. Compared to manual picking methods, these automated systems can increase throughput by 30% to 40%.

## The Future of Logistics Technology: Emerging Technologies and Trends Transforming the Logistics Industry

![](https://grydd.com/storage/2024/06/Technology-02.png)

## 1. Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning

Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) are at the forefront of technological advancements in logistics. AI-powered systems can analyze vast amounts of data to optimize routes, predict demand, and improve inventory management. Machine learning algorithms enhance decision-making by identifying patterns and trends that humans might miss.

One significant application of AI in logistics is predictive analytics. By analyzing historical data and current market conditions, AI can forecast demand more accurately, helping companies to manage inventory better and reduce waste. Additionally, AI-powered chatbots and virtual assistants are improving customer service by providing real-time updates and handling routine inquiries efficiently.

## 2. Internet of Things (IoT)

The Internet of Things (IoT) is another transformative technology in logistics. IoT devices, such as sensors and RFID tags, enable real-time tracking and monitoring of goods throughout the supply chain. This connectivity provides greater visibility and control, allowing companies to monitor the condition and location of their shipments.

IoT technology also enhances predictive maintenance for transportation fleets. Sensors on vehicles can detect potential issues before they become critical, reducing downtime and maintenance costs. Moreover, IoT-enabled smart warehouses use automated systems to track inventory levels, streamline operations, and minimize errors.

## 3. Blockchain Technology

Blockchain technology is gaining traction in the logistics industry due to its potential to enhance transparency and security. A blockchain is a decentralized ledger that records transactions across multiple computers, ensuring that the data is secure and tamper-proof.

In logistics, blockchain can be used to create an immutable record of the entire supply chain process. This transparency helps in verifying the authenticity of goods, reducing fraud, and ensuring compliance with regulations. Smart contracts, which are self-executing contracts with the terms of the agreement directly written into code, can automate and streamline transactions, reducing administrative costs and delays.

![](https://grydd.com/storage/2024/06/Technology-03.png)

## 4. Autonomous Vehicles and Drones

Autonomous vehicles and drones are set to revolutionize transportation and delivery in the logistics industry. Self-driving trucks can operate continuously without the need for breaks, significantly reducing delivery times and costs. Companies like Tesla and Waymo are already testing autonomous trucks on public roads, and widespread adoption is on the horizon.

Drones offer a solution for last-mile delivery, especially in remote or congested areas. Companies like Amazon and UPS are experimenting with drone delivery systems that can quickly and efficiently transport small packages directly to customers’ doorsteps. These technologies not only enhance delivery speed but also reduce the environmental impact of logistics operations.

## 5. Robotics and Automation

Robotics and automation are transforming warehouse operations, making them more efficient and less labor-intensive. Automated guided vehicles (AGVs) and robotic arms can handle repetitive tasks such as picking, packing, and sorting, increasing productivity and reducing the risk of human error.

Advanced robotics systems equipped with AI can adapt to changes in the warehouse environment, improving flexibility and scalability. For example, collaborative robots, or cobots, work alongside human workers, enhancing productivity without replacing jobs. These innovations are particularly valuable in addressing labor shortages and meeting the growing demand for faster order fulfillment.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Technologies like AI, IoT, and blockchain are reshaping supply chains, enhancing efficiency and visibility. Companies must adopt these innovations for competitive advantage and operational resilience.

Consumers increasingly demand transparency in sourcing and production. Blockchain technology enables traceability, ensuring that companies can verify product origins and maintain accountability throughout the supply chain.

Organizations are moving towards digital platforms for better data integration and real-time analytics. Cloud solutions enhance collaboration, streamline operations, and support agile decision-making in supply chain management.
